coredata.dc:title: The influence of annealing treatments on the microstructural and optical properties of a-SiC x :H films embedded with carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Lihua
Peng, Yu
Wang, Tao
Tian, Haiyan
Xiao, Ting
Xiang, Peng
Sun, Yihua
Tan, Xinyu
coredata.dc:description: In order to obtain the carbon nanodots dispersed in the solid state films, carbon-rich amorphous hydrogenated silicon carbide (a-SiC x :H) films were prepared by PECVD method and were subsequently annealed at Argon protective environment for 30 min with different annealing temperatures (Ta). Raman spectra show that the amorphous carbon nanodots were formed within the a-SiC x :H films as Ta is equal to and higher than 600 °C. Meanwhile, the decomposition and transformation from sp3 C to sp2 C of the carbon nanodots can be inferred by the Raman spectra with increasing Ta. XPS analysis suggest that the near-surface oxidation of the films become strong as Ta > 600 °C which can be contributed to the chemical bond reconstruction and the formation of carbon nanodots within the films. The decrease of the optical band gap derived from the measured absorption spectra of the films can be attributed to the formation and graphitization of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films. HRTEM measurements indicate that the enhancement of the areal density and the size reduction of the carbon nanodots take place within the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta. However, the dispersion of the carbon nanodots embedded in the a-SiC x :H films is still very bad compared with that of the CQDs dispersed in chemical solvents. PL measurements show that the PL signals may originate from the related defect states rather than the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
PECVD
a-SiC x :H films
Annealing treatment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The Raman spectra of the as-deposited and annealed a-SiC x :H films over the range from 800 to 2000 cm-1 and (b) the intensity ratios of the D and G peaks (ID/IG) with increasing Ta.
The typical XPS full scanning spectral lines of the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta.
The typical high-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Si 2p and (b) C 1s peaks of the a-SiC x :H films with different Ta.
(a) Transmittance spectra, (b) absorption spectra and (c) Tauc plots of the a-SiC x :H films with different Ta, (d) the dependence of optical band gap on Ta.

The variations in the surface color of the fused quartz plate substrates with different Ta. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The bright-field plan view HRTEM images of the a-SiC x :H films annealed at Ta = 650, 700 and 750 °C with two rulers of length 100 nm and 20 nm, respectively. Inset shows corresponding SAED patterns.

(a) The PL spectra of the as-deposited and annealed films with different Ta, the PL spectra of the as-deposited film (b) and the films annealed at 300 °C (c), 400 °C (d) and 500 °C (e) together with the fitting results using four Gaussian peaks, (f) the variations of the PL peak (P1, P2, P3 and P4) positions with different Ta.

head.ce:title: The influence of annealing treatments on the microstructural and optical properties of a-SiC x :H films embedded with carbon nanodots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electrical Engineering & New Energy
Hubei Provincial Collaborative Innovation Center for New Energy Microgrid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to obtain the carbon nanodots dispersed in the solid state films, carbon-rich amorphous hydrogenated silicon carbide (a-SiC x :H) films were prepared by PECVD method and were subsequently annealed at Argon protective environment for 30 min with different annealing temperatures (Ta). Raman spectra show that the amorphous carbon nanodots were formed within the a-SiC x :H films as Ta is equal to and higher than 600 °C. Meanwhile, the decomposition and transformation from sp3 C to sp2 C of the carbon nanodots can be inferred by the Raman spectra with increasing Ta. XPS analysis suggest that the near-surface oxidation of the films become strong as Ta > 600 °C which can be contributed to the chemical bond reconstruction and the formation of carbon nanodots within the films. The decrease of the optical band gap derived from the measured absorption spectra of the films can be attributed to the formation and graphitization of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films. HRTEM measurements indicate that the enhancement of the areal density and the size reduction of the carbon nanodots take place within the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta. However, the dispersion of the carbon nanodots embedded in the a-SiC x :H films is still very bad compared with that of the CQDs dispersed in chemical solvents. PL measurements show that the PL signals may originate from the related defect states rather than the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide films have wide and potential applications in the fields of microelectronic and optoelectronic devices, and have been paid much attention due to their varied optical bandgap, good thermal stability, high hardness and excellent elasticity, etc [1,2]. Moreover, it can be used as dielectric matrix for the growth of silicon quantum dots [2–4]. Generally, the silicon quantum dots can be precipitated within Si-rich a-SiC x :H films in situ way or by annealing treatments [3,4]. Recently, carbon nanostructure or carbon nanodots were observed in carbon-rich amorphous hydrogenated silicon carbide (a-SiC x :H) films [5–7]. These suggest that it is possible to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within a-SiC x :H films just like silicon quantum dots precipitation within Si-rich silicon carbide matrix. It is well known that CQDs are a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials with interesting chemical and physical properties [8,9]. Due to their unique electronic and optical properties [10], they can be used in a wide range of applications including optronics, catalysis and sensors, etc [11]. At present, the majority of CQDs synthesis methods reported by researchers are chemical synthetic strategies such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, photo-fenton reactions, electrochemistry, metal-graphite intercalation, carbohydrate dehydration and organic synthesis approaches, etc [12,13]. However, the CQDs synthesized by above methods are usually dispersed in chemical solvents or water [13]. Meanwhile, the surfaces of the CQDs are also often modified by some uncontrollable oxygen functional groups. Thus, these preparation methods limit the CQDs applications in the fields of solid state optoelectronic devices, such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs), solar cells and photodetectors, etc. Moreover, the CQDs obtained through drying chemical solvents are prone to aggregate and make fluorescence become quenched [14]. Therefore, for the applications of the CQDs in the fileds of solid state optoelectronic devices, it will be meaningful to fabricate CQDs dispersed well in solid state films.

In order to overcome the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of CQDs and to realize the application of CQDs in solid state optoelectronic devices, researchers have dispersed CQDs in silicone [15], starch [16], and other high molecular polymers [17]. However, there is little research about CQDs which are embedded within the solid state inorganic films. Moreover, if the preparation process of CQDs is compatible with the traditional semiconductor technology, it will be more meaningful for the application of CQDs in solid state optoelectronic devices. In recent decades, plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method is one of the widely used fabrication process of semiconductor devices. Therefore, in this work, the carbon-rich a-SiC x :H films will be fabricated by PECVD method through decomposition of methane and silane. Then, we try to precipitate CQDs within the prepared a-SiC x :H films by annealing treatments in argon protective atmosphere. Furthermore, the surfaces of the CQDs prepared by this method will not be easily modified by the uncontrollable oxygen functional groups. It is well known that the CQDs tend to aggregate during baking chemical solvents and the photoluminescence (PL) properties can be tuned by changing its size [10]. Does the CQDs embedded in the a-SiC x :H films have the same agglomeration and luminescence characteristics as those in solvent? In addition, for amorphous carbon films, many research works have reported that the transformation of sp3 hybridization carbon to sp2 hybridization carbon will take place during annealing treatments [18]. And, thermal stability of the a-C:H films will also be affected by annealing treatments [18]. Does the CQDs embedded in a-SiC x :H films will also undergo graphitization transformation with annealing treatment? What about the thermal stability of the CQDs embedded in the a-SiC x :H films under the conditions of the annealing treatments? In this work, the above questions will be discussed.

The a-SiC x :H films were simultaneously deposited on n-type Si (100) wafer, crystalline KBr and fused quartz plate substrates by PECVD (radio frequency 13.56 MHz) method using a mixture of diluted silane (90 vol % H2 + 10 vol % SiH4) and pure CH4 (purity 99.9995%) as working gas with a RF power of 120 W (about 380 mW/cm2). The cleaning method of quartz plates and n-type Si wafers has been described in Ref. [18]. Prior to deposition, the PECVD working chamber was pumped down to a base pressure below 10−4 Pa. During deposition, the chamber pressure was kept at around 70 Pa and the substrates temperature was controlled at about 200 °C. The flow rates of the diluted silane and pure CH4 were set at around 10 and 17 sccm, respectively. One thing to note here is that to ensure that the two kinds of gases can be mixed evenly in the PECVD working chamber, the two kinds of gases first were collected in certain proportion outside the PECVD working chamber and then were transported to the working chamber together. To obtain proper film thickness, the deposition time was set at 1 h. After the film deposition, the as-deposited films will be cut into several pieces, and these pieces were subsequently annealed in a quartz-tube furnace at a high purity Argon (purity 99.9995%) protective environment for 30 min. The annealing temperatures (Ta) were set at 300, 400, 500, 600, 650, 700 and 750 °C, respectively. After annealing, the cooling method of the sample is the same as that in reference [18].

The a-SiC x :H films deposited on the n-type Si (100) wafers were mainly used for the room temperature measurement of Raman spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The microstructures of the films were examined by Raman spectra (LabRAM HR Evolution) with excitation by an Argon ion laser (excitation wavelength: 532 nm and laser power: 5 mW). The compositions and chemical bonding states of the films were surveyed by using an X-ray spec-trophotometer (Thermo Fisher EscaLab 250Xi) with a monochromatic Al Kα source. The PL properties of the films were captured by a spectro-fluorimeter (Hitachi Model F-4500, excitation wavelength: 350 nm). Optical absorption and transmittance spectra of the a-SiC x :H films deposited on the fused quartz plate substrates were recorded by a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV-2550v, wavelength region: 300–800 nm). During UV-Vis spectroscopy measurement, a blank fused quartz plate was placed on the reference beam. The optical band gap Eg of the films can be calculated from the optical absorption spectra by the Tauc equation [1,19]. With increasing Ta, the formation, size variations and distribution of the CQDs within the a-SiC x :H films deposited on the crystalline KBr substrates will be observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30 operated at 300 KV). Before the HRTEM characterization, firstly, the a-SiC x :H thin films were separated from the KBr substrates by soaking in deionized water, secondly, the separated films will be dried and then annealed. The Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) patterns of the CQDs were also recorded for estimating the crystalline state of the CQDs.

The evolution of the microstructural properties of the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta can be observed by the Raman measurements. The Raman scattering signals of the as-deposited and annealed a-SiC x :H films are shown in Fig. 1 (a). It can be easily found that no any carbon-related Raman signals in the range of 800–2000 cm−1 appeared as Ta < 600 °C [20,21]. The absence of carbon-related Raman scattering may be caused by the low content of the carbon nanodots. Actually, in this work, it may be more appropriate to label the CQDs as the carbon nanodots due to the bigger sizes for the most of them that will be presented by the following HRTEM measurements.

At Ta = 600 °C, a very broad band centered at about 1430 cm−1 can be found. Such Raman scattering signal usually was assigned to amorphous carbon nanodots and/or carbon network attached with Si [22,23]. At Ta = 650, 700 and 750 °C, two separated peaks of the Raman bands which centered at around 1340 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 can be clearly observed. Generally, the band peaks at around 1340 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are referred to as the D (disordered) and G (graphitic) peaks of graphite-like local structure of amorphous carbon, respectively [21–23]. Thus, it is reasonable to believe that the more amorphous carbon nanodots were formed within the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta. In Fig. 1(a), it can also be found that the intensity of carbon-related Raman scattering reached its maximum value at Ta = 650 °C, and then began to decline with the further increase of annealing temperature and finally reached its minimum value at Ta = 750 °C. The appearance of the obvious D and G peaks after annealing at Ta > 600 °C indicated that the ordering of the graphite-like local coordination in carbon nanodots was enhanced and structural defects in the carbon nanodots may decrease [5,24].

The intensity increase of the carbon-related Raman scattering may suggest that Raman inactive sp3 hybridized carbon bonds transform to Raman active sp2 hybridized carbon bonds with the release of H under the conditions of increasing Ta. Namely, the graphitization of carbon nanodots increases with increasing Ta [5,24]. Meanwhile, the intensity increase of the carbon-related Raman scattering signals can also be attributed to the precipitation of more carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films during the annealing treatments. The intensity decrease of the carbon-related Raman scattering after annealing at Ta > 650 °C can be attributed to the thermal decomposition of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films under the conditions of the annealing treatments [18]. In a previous research work (reference [18]) we found that the Raman spectral intensity of the hydrogenated amorphous carbon films start to reduce at Ta > 400 °C, namely, the thermal decomposition of the hydrogenated amorphous carbon films took place at Ta > 400 °C. In this work, the thermal decomposition of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films appeared at Ta > 650 °C. The increase of the thermal decomposition temperature may indicate that the silicon modification on carbon nanodot surface can improve the thermal stability of the carbon nanodot within the a-SiC x :H films. Or, in other words, compared with hydrogenated amorphous carbon films, the carbon nanodots embedded in a-SiC x :H films have better thermal stability.

Fig. 1(b) shows the variations of the intensity ratios (ID/IG, the height ratio of the two peaks) of D and G peaks with increasing Ta. It can be found that the ratio of ID/IG increases with increasing Ta. Generally, the sp3 hybridization carbon content in the hydrogenated amorphous carbon films can be indirectly inferred by the ratio of ID/IG. The higher ID/IG ratio indicates the lower sp3 hybridization carbon content percentage (or the higher sp2 hybridization carbon content percentage) in the hydrogenated amorphous carbon films [21,25]. Thus, it can be judged that the sp2 hybridization carbon content increases within the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta, and it also indicates that the sp3 hybridized carbon bonds transform to sp2 hybridized carbon bonds with increasing Ta. These results are consistent with the front discussion of the increase of the Raman spectral intensity. Moreover, researchers have found that the ratio of ID/IG is inversely proportional to graphite cluster size in the a-SiC films [22,26,27]. A. V. Vasin et al. also reported that the carbon nanodots become larger with decreasing the ID/IG ratio for a carbon cluster size larger than 1.2–2.5 nm, but the carbon nanodots will become smaller with decreasing the ID/IG ratio for crystallites smaller than 1.2–2.5 nm [23]. In this work, it can be observed that the carbon nanodots were amorphous and their sizes were larger than 1.2–2.5 nm by the following HRTEM measurements. In Fig. 1(b), it was shown that the ratio of the ID/IG increases with increasing Ta from 650 to 750 °C. On the base of the above results, it can be deduced that the size of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films decreases with the increase of Ta from 650 to 750 °C. Such judgement can be confirmed by the following HRTEM measurements. In addition, A. V. Vasin et al. found that a broad single Raman scattering signal without the separated D and G peaks can be used as the evidence of the presence of very small (about 1 nm) and highly disordered carbon nanodots [23]. In Fig. 1(a), a broad single Raman scattering signal without the separated D and G peaks can be observed at Ta = 600 °C. Thus, based on the work of reference [23] it indicated that the sizes of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films were very small at Ta = 600 °C. However, with the further increase of Ta, more carbon nanodots precipitated and then grown up. Therefore, the separated D and G peaks appeared at Ta > 600 °C. It is also known that the thermal decomposition of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films appeared at Ta > 650 °C. Thus, it is reasonable to believe that the precipitation, growth and decomposition of the carbon nanodots coexist within the a-SiC x :H films during the annealing treatments at Ta > 650 °C. It have shown that the size of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films decreases with the increase of Ta from 650 to 750 °C. This suggests that, compared with the precipitation and growth, the thermal decomposition of the carbon nanodots during the annealing treatments is predominant process when Ta is higher than 650 °C.

The variations of the chemical bonds and compositions of the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta can be given by XPS measurements. The typical XPS full scanning spectral lines of the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta are shown in Fig. 2 . In this figure, only Si, C and O elements can be detected [22]. In addition, it can be easily observed that the intensity of the O 1s peak obviously increases with increasing Ta as Ta > 500 °C, however, the intensity variations of the C 1s peak is exactly the opposite of that of the O 1s peak. It is well known that the XPS measurement only characterizes a few nm at the external near-surface of the films [28]. Thus, it seem to indicate that the Ta has a great influence on the chemical bonds and compositions of the near-surface of the a-SiC x :H films. Namely, for the near-surface of the a-SiC x :H films, the surface oxidation become strong and the carbon content of the near-surface decrease significantly with increasing Ta as Ta is higher than 500 °C. Does such oxidation also operate in the bulk of these films? In fact, it can be inferred from the subsequent UV-Vis measurement and analysis that the oxidation only occurs at the near-surface of the a-SiC x :H films rather than the bulk of the films. If the oxidation occurs through the bulk of the films, the introduction of the O will increase the optical band gap of the a-SiC x :H films, however, it reduces with increasing Ta. Although it is not clear whether the surface oxidation takes place during or after annealing treatments, it is believed that the near-surface oxidation is related to the chemical bond reconstruction and the formation of carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films. The oxygen may originate from the residual oxygen gas in the annealing chamber or can be attributed to the oxidization of the films in air before the XPS measurement [24,29].

To get detailed informations about the chemical bond reconstruction and the carbon cluster formation within the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta, the typical high-resolution XPS spectra of Si 2p and C 1s peaks of the a-SiC x :H films with different Ta are presented in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively. In Fig. 3 (a), it can be clearly found that the Si 2p peak shifts from about 101.4 eV for the as-deposited film to 102.5 eV at Ta = 600 °C and then was finally fixed at around 103.6 eV with Ta = 750 °C. It was indicated that the Si 2p peak gradually shifts to higher energy with increasing Ta. Generally, for the Si 2p photoelectron, the peaks centered at 101.4, 102.5 and 103.6 eV can be attributed to Si–C, Si–C–O and Si–O chemical bonds, respectively [29,30]. It is well known that the related Si dangling bonds can be generated during and/or after annealing treatments and the Si dangling bonds on the a-SiC x :H films can easily combine with O and form Si–O bonds [29]. In addition, A. M. Hartel et al. have reported that, the more silicon incorporated in Si x C 1-x :H layer, the more oxygen can oxidize on the Si x C 1-x :H layer surface, this will lead to the increase of the Si–O bond density [31]. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that the Si–C bonds near the surface of the a-SiC x :H films are gradually replaced by Si–O bonds with the increase of Ta. In Fig. 3 (b), it can be easily observed that the intensities of C 1s peaks drop obviously down as Ta is equal to and higher than 600 °C. Moreover, at 600 °C, the widths of the Si 2p and C 1s peaks seem to be significantly broader than that of the two peaks at other annealing temperatures. It mean that Ta = 600 °C may be a critical annealing temperature for the a-SiC x :H films annealed in this work. At Ta = 600 °C, the a-SiC x :H films may contain more species of chemical bonds, thus, the two peaks become more broader. Such as, by using Gaussian multi-peak fitting, the Si 2p peak can be decomposed into Si–C (around 101.4 eV), Si–C–O (around 102.5 eV) and Si–O (around 103.6 eV) peaks, and the C 1s peak can be consisted of C–Si (around 283.5 eV), sp2 C (around 284.4 eV), sp3 C (around 285.5 eV), C–O–H (around 286.4 eV) and C–O (around 286.3 eV) peaks, etc [29].

It is well known that the a-SiC x :H films contain a lot of hydrogen-terminated bonds and some weak C–H and Si–H bonds will be broken and reconstructed with annealing treatments [5]. Thus, more Si and C dangling bonds will be generated and the effusion of hydrogen will be thermally activated with increasing Ta. Therefore, it is possible that the carbon nanodots can be formed within the a-SiC x :H films during the annealing treatments [32]. Of course, during and/or after annealing treatments, the C dangling bonds on the surface of the a-SiC x :H films also can combine with O and form C–O bonds and lead to the formation of volatile CO and/or CO2 molecules [23,29]. The creation of more CC bonds within hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbon films with annealing treatments at 600 °C has been reported [32]. In this work, the formed carbon nanodots within the annealed a-SiC x :H films have been confirmed by the Raman spectra and will be further verified by the following HRTEM measurements. A. V. Vasin et al. found that the oxidation of hydrogenated silicon-carbon alloy layers is critically related to the appearance of graphite-like clusters and the oxidation of graphitic carbon by oxygen is thermodynamically favorable at temperatures above 500–600 °C [5]. Meanwhile, they reported that the amorphous carbon phase embedded in the hydrogenated silicon-carbon alloy layers began to react with oxygen at 400 °C and became very strong above 500 °C [5]. Moreover, the related hydrogen-terminated bonds will make the a-SiC x :H films contain more atomic-scale voids and increase the porosity of the a-SiC x :H films [5,32]. W. K. Choi et al. reported that the 600 °C annealed hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide film is more porous than the as-prepared film and the annealed film oxidized faster (about 100%) than the as-prepared film [32]. On the base of these results, in this work, the formed carbon nanodots can be oxidized and removed from the near-surface of the annealed a-SiC x :H films in the form of volatile carbon oxides. Such a process further increases the porosity of the near-surface of the annealed a-SiC x :H films, thus further promoting oxidation [5]. Indeed, these are well agree with the decrease of the Raman intensity and the increase of the ratio of ID/IG as shown in Fig. 1. Meanwhile, these are also consistent with the reduction of the carbon content and the enhancement in O 1s peak intensity as shown in Fig. 2.

The evolutions of the optical transmission and absorption properties of the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta can be obtained by the UV-VIS spectroscopy measurement. Meanwhile, the optical band gap Eg of the a-SiC x :H films can be derived from the measured absorption spectra of the films deposited on the fused quartz plate substrates. The Eg was deduced from the known Tauc formula by extrapolating the linear behavior of (αhv) 1/2 as a function of photo energy hv [23]. The transmission spectra of the a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and the obvious interference oscillation fringes can be observed as Ta is lower than 600 °C. It has shown that at Ta = 600 °C, for the Raman, XPS and UV-Vis spectra, firstly, the related Raman scattering signals of the amorphous carbon nanodots began to arise, secondly, the near-surface oxidation and the decomposition of the carbon nanodots can be inferred through the XPS spectra analysis, finally, the interference fringes of the transmission spectra of the a-SiC x :H films disappeared. These may mean that at Ta = 600 °C, the composition and microstructure of the a-SiC x :H film will change greatly.

In Fig. 4 (a), it also can be easily observed that the optical transmission characteristics of the a-SiC x :H films change very little as Ta is lower than and equal to 500 °C, however, the transparency of the a-SiC x :H films reduce notably as Ta increases from 600 to 750 °C. The absorption spectra of the a-SiC x :H films with different Ta are shown in Fig. 4 (b). Obviously, with increasing Ta, the variations of the optical absorption characteristics of the a-SiC x :H films are consistent with that of the transparency of the a-SiC x :H films. The Tauc plots and the Eg of the a-SiC x :H films derived from the measured absorption spectra of the films deposited on the fused quartz plate substrates are given in Fig. 4 (c) and (d), respectively. It can be clearly observed that the Eg gradually decrease with increasing Ta. At Ta > 600 °C, the Eg of the a-SiC x :H films is lower than 1 eV, which is far less than that of stoichiometric a-SiC (about 2.3 eV) [33]. Thus, it is reasonable to believe that the decrease of the Eg can be attributed to the formation and graphitization of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films annealed at different Ta [18,23,29], which have been confirmed by the Raman scattering measurements. The formation and graphitization of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films also can be indirectly verified from the variations in the surface color of the annealed a-SiC x :H films deposited on the fused quartz plate substrates as shown in Fig. 5 . In this figure, it can be easily observed that the surface color of the a-SiC x :H films gradually become dark with increasing Ta and is completely black at Ta = 750 °C. This shown that the a-SiC x :H films gradually become carbonization with increasing Ta. Moreover, the effusion of the hydrogen out of the a-SiC x :H films with annealing treatments will also result in the decrease of the Eg Ref. [23]. It is generally agreed that the a-SiC x :H films contain various chemical bonds, such as Si–H, Si–C and C–H bonds and sp2-coordinated C–C bonds [23]. Generally, the optical band gap gained from the Tauc formula is an averaged value for the total material of the film. The Si–H, Si–C and C–H bonds can make the Eg of the a-SiC x :H films become large, whereas, the sp2-coordinated C–C bonds can form very low bandgap [23]. Therefore, the lower relative concentration of hydrogen bonds will result in the lower optical band gap calculated by using the Tauc formula [23]. In the XPS spectra, it have introduced that the oxidation only occurs at the near-surface of the a-SiC x :H films rather than the bulk of the films. Because the related O bonds can make the Eg become large, if the oxidation occurs through the bulk of the films, the introduction of the O will increase the Eg of the a-SiC x :H films rather than decrease the Eg. Therefore, in this work, it is reasonable to believe that the oxidation only occurs at the near-surface of the a-SiC x :H film with annealing treatment and there is no oxidation inside the film.

The bright-field plan view HRTEM images of the a-SiC x :H films annealed at Ta = 650, 700 and 750 °C with two rulers of length 100 nm and 20 nm are shown in Fig. 6 and insets present the corresponding SAED patterns. The HRTEM images of the a-SiC x :H films annealed at Ta = 650, 700 and 750 °C clearly show the presence of the carbon nanodots embedded in the a-SiC x :H films and the SAED patterns confirm the amorphous characteristics of the carbon nanodots. At Ta = 650 °C, it can be found that the carbon nanodots are not only larger size, but also each of the carbon nanodot is connected together. With increasing Ta from 650 to 750 °C, the sizes of the carbon nanodots gradually reduce and even some of the carbon nanodots that the sizes are smaller than 10 nm can be easily found. Moreover, more separated carbon nanodots also can be found and this suggests that the dispersion of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films can be improved by increasing Ta. Meanwhile, the areal density of the carbon nanodots seems to increase with increasing Ta by visual inspection. The growth and decomposition of the carbon nanodots with annealing treatments have been confirmed by the previous Raman and XPS measurement. It can be inferred that the enhancement of the areal density and the size reduction of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films may be caused by a combination of the above two factors. It is worthy to note that the CQDs dispersed in chemical solvents are well-distributed and tend to aggregate by drying chemical solvents. In this work, as shown in Fig. 6, although the dispersion of the carbon nanodots embedded in the a-SiC x :H films can be improved by annealing treatments, their dispersion is still very bad compared with that of the CQDs dispersed in chemical solvents. It suggested that the carbon nanodots embedded in the a-SiC x :H films also have strong aggregation property.

The PL spectra of the as-deposited and annealed a-SiC x :H films are shown in Fig. 7 (a). It can be observed that the PL spectra of the films are very broad and cover all visible spectra range from 450 to 750 nm. Moreover, although the annealing treatments at Ta = 500 °C make the PL intensity reach the maximum, the PL intensity of the annealed films rapidly reduce at Ta = 600 °C and then the PL signals are almost disappeared as the Ta increase from 650 to 750 °C. To precisely ensure the PL mechanisms of the films, the PL spectra of the as-deposited and the annealed films with the Ta = 300, 400 and 500 °C have been decomposed into the four peaks with the best Gaussian fitting in the range of 450–750 nm, as shown in Fig. 7 (b)–(e). The four peaks are labeled as P1, P2, P3 and P4 as shown in Fig. 7 (b)–(e) and they locate at around 470, 520, 570 and 635 nm as shown in Fig. 7 (f), respectively. In Fig. 7 (f), it can be found that the positions of the four peaks are almost invariable with increasing Ta.

Based on the results of the Raman spectra and the HRTEM measurements a large number of carbon nanodots are appeared as the Ta is equal to and higher than 600 °C. Therefore, according to the variations of the PL signals with increasing Ta, it may be improper to contribute the PL to the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films. It is well known that the peak positions of the PL bands originated from the defect-related states can’t be easily controlled and depend only on the defect state energy level [19,34,35]. In our previous work [19], the 470 nm (2.64 eV) PL band has been attributed to the luminescence centers of Si substrates and/or the Si-related defect states in the hydrogenated Si-rich silicon carbide/amorphous carbon nano-multi-layer films. In this work, the PL peak of 470 nm was also observed and its peak position can’t be shifted by annealing treatments with different temperatures. This is very similar to our previous work [19], therefore, the peak P1 located at around 470 nm may be attributed to the luminescence centers of Si substrates and/or the Si-related defect states in the a-SiC x :H films [19]. The rest of the three PL peaks may originate from the related defect states in the a-SiC x :H films. The similar changed PL characteristics with increasing annealing temperature have been reported by A. V. Vasin et al. [23]. They found that the PL intensity was related to the nonradiative recombination center concentration of the paramagnetic defects. Under the conditions of the low temperature (Ta < 600 °C) annealing treatments, the atomic hydrogen can be released from the weak bonds and then passivate the paramagnetic defects in the a-SiC x :H films [23]. Therefore, the PL intensity can be enhanced with increasing Ta. However, by the high-temperature (Ta > 600 °C) annealing treatments, the effusion of the hydrogen out of the a-SiC x :H films will take place and the carbon nanodots can be formed. Moreover, the transformation of sp3 carbon nanodots to sp2 carbon nanodots may also happen. These have been confirmed by the Raman spectra and the HRTEM measurements. The dehydrogenation and the enhancement of the sp2 carbon nanodots can increase the concentration of non-radiative recombination centers of the carbon related paramagnetic defects [23]. Thus, the PL can be quenched under the conditions of the high-temperature (Ta > 600 °C) annealing treatments.

The influence of the annealing treatments on the microstructural and optical properties of the a-SiC x :H films embedded with the carbon nanodots prepared by PECVD method from the decomposition of a mixture of diluted silane (90 vol % H2 + 10 vol % SiH4) and pure CH4 (purity 99.9995%) have been studied. The microstructural and optical properties of the as-deposited and the annealed films with different Ta have been measured by the Raman, XPS, UV-Vis, HRTEM and PL spectroscopy techniques. The Raman spectra show that the precipitation, growth and decomposition of the carbon nanodots coexist within the a-SiC x :H films during the annealing treatments at Ta > 650 °C. It can be found that the formed carbon nanodots can be oxidized and removed from the near-surface of the annealed a-SiC x :H films with increasing Ta by XPS measurements. The decrease of the Eg of the a-SiC x :H films can be attributed to the formation and graphitization of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films. HRTEM shown that, with increasing Ta from 650 to 750 °C, the sizes of the carbon nanodots gradually reduce and even some of the carbon nanodots that the sizes are smaller than 10 nm can be easily found. Moreover, the dispersion of the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films also can be improved by increasing Ta. Meanwhile, the areal density of the carbon nanodots seems to increase with increasing Ta. However, the dispersion of the carbon nanodots embedded in the a-SiC x :H films is still very bad compared with that of the CQDs dispersed in chemical solvents. According to the variations of the PL signals with increasing Ta, it may be inappropriate to contribute the PL to the carbon nanodots within the a-SiC x :H films and PL peaks may originate from the related defect states in the films.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61604087 and U1765105), the State Scholarship fund of the China Scholarship Council (CSC201708420285) and the Research Fund for Excellent Dissertation of China Three Gorges University (2018SSPY058).
